speaker: interviewer
text: No, we're not rolling yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some of us restricted our intake of water. We needed some water. What we would do, put our hands in the ocean-- that's how close we were on the ocean, of the ocean in this barge. And we'd wet our lips and our mouth just enough to take in some moisture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I ask you a question? Who was navigating the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: The boat was navigated by a tugboat of German SS men.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So there's no SS men actually on the barge with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no SS on the barge.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were at the-- on the tug boat. In any event, we must have been on the-- on the boat, on the barge, for probably about five days or six days. And the boat anchored one night. Unbeknownst to us, the tug boat disappeared with the SS. After several days, five or six days, we arrived at a point where the tugboat anchored. They put an anchor on our barge. And it was late in the evening. The tug boat disappeared. We, of course, were wondering what was happening and where we were. But we could see that there was land not too far from where we were anchored. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Didn't pay too much attention to it. During the night, we heard tremendous cannon fire. And we realized that we had to be somewhere near the front. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It turned out that that was the night of May the 4th, when British troops were advancing into the area which was Neustadt Schleswig-Holstein in Germany, where we were anchored. But it was not known to us at that time, while we were still on the boat, where we were. Morning came. The tug boat was still gone. The SS was still gone. And quite a few of the people on the-- on the barge went overboard and swam onto land. I couldn't swim, so obviously I stayed on the barge. Daylight broke about 8 o'clock, maybe early, 7o'clock. The SS came back with the tug boat and realized and also saw that quite a few people swimming on land. They went back on land, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] rounded up whoever was on land, marched them into the water, and killed them all, shot them all. Must have been about 40 or 50 people who had made it on land. Meantime, we're still on the-- on the barge. The noise of the cannon fire is getting closer. Airplanes are appearing overhead with British insignias on it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now the SS in a tug boat disappears completely. They're nowhere to be seen. And we are sitting on this barge, not knowing, should anyone else try to get onto land or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 9 o'clock, aside from the cannon fire, we're hearing tremendous of-- amount of noise. Couldn't figure out what it was. Didn't know. No idea. But we knew that the end was near. Approximately 9:30, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we saw British tanks rolling along the road approximately 500 feet from where we were anchored in the water. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was no SS. And we were free to make noise and identify ourselves. Seemingly, some of the people who had escaped earlier had gotten away and were intercepted, or had intercepted these tanks, told the British tank crew that there was a ship out there with us. And they stopped in line with where we were anchored. There were several small boats laying on land. Manned the boats, rowed to the boat, to the-- to the barge where we were in. And of course, they identified themselves and said they were British soldiers. They were liberating us, which it really was difficult to understand. It was impossible to comprehend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Being liberated, what does it mean? You're-- you're cautious. You are afraid. Is it true? What does it mean? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Is it possible? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: They brought us on land. And from land, by that time they had a mass of trucks, British trucks, had come to that site. There were several Red Cross trucks there that picked us up, gave us some food to eat, and took us to a hospital. That was, of course, our-- our liberation. I don't think anyone realized [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that we were liberated. Took us to this hospital. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll never forget this. There were beds. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And here am I, [INAUDIBLE], who had survived with-- with me. And we were looking at each other and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] are we dreaming or is-- is this real? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Obviously, it was real. Unfortunately, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some of our friends, my friends, were liberated with me, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] did not make it past that hospital, died in the hospital, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just weren't strong enough. They were strong enough to live, to survive, but not strong enough to live. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's a terrible thing to see friends who you had gone through this terrible experience with. You know, the feeling of loss of friends after liberation to me was worse than the loss of friends during the Holocaust, during the concentration camp days, because you made it so long, you suffered so much [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to be free, to be a human being again, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then you cannot enjoy [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] your struggles, what you struggled for, what you fought for. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I consider that the loss of two or possibly three people [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as great a loss, other than my family, of friends which I lost during the Holocaust, more than those which didn't make it up to that point.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, life begins on May 3rd, 1945, approximately 10 o'clock in the morning, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] life without family, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] life with-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] without a meaning, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just life to live. Does it make sense? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Fortunately, we had some very good counseling. There were some Jewish chaplains available from the British army, some social service people who helped us through the first few days. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And a strange thing happened to all of us, to me. I mean, I'm going to speak about me. After two days being in the hospital, I was able to walk and get out. Never forget this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I walked the hospital grounds on the street. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I hadn't mentioned that, but I'm sure you're aware of the Jews were not allowed to walk on the sidewalk [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the late '30s and early '40s. And of course, while we were in ghetto and concentration camps, we always had to walk on the street. Jews were not allowed to walk on the sidewalks. And here I'm walking the hospital grounds on the street. And somebody tapped me on the shoulder and said, Bert, Luds, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you're a free man. You don't have to walk on the street anymore. And I believe it took me about three or four weeks to realize [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I-- I was a free person. Just the significance of walking on a sidewalk was difficult to comprehend [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after you have been treated like an animal and walking on the street all these years. But eventually, I realized that I was a free man now. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] After a while, of course, I got used to the idea of being a free man. Number one, the food I was eating indicated that I was free. I had no one beating me up, ordering me around. But of course, I had lost-- the most precious thing in my life was my family. Nobody was there anymore. But I always had hope that my sister was still alive, because the last time I'd seen her was in Stuthoff, and she was in pretty good condition yet at that time, good enough to survive. From the hospital, I went to a sanatorium to recuperate. I weighed in at that time at 72 pounds. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Needless to say, I was skin and bones. In a period of about four months, May, June, July, August, September, I recuperated enough to be able to be on my own. At least I felt I could be on my own. And since I had no family, nevertheless hoped that my sister had survived, I proceeded to go back to my hometown, Gemunden. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Big surprise I arrived there. Obviously, nobody expected me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went back to my house, my birth house, the house I was born and raised in, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which had been used during the war as a home for Russian prisoners, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that housed Russian prisoners in that house. It was completely demolished and torn apart. And it was no place for me to live in. In any event, I went to the mayor's office, townhouse, mayor's office. And I introduced myself, who I was. And it so happens that this man was installed by the American occupation troops-- this is an American occupied territory-- as the mayor of the town, who was a very good friend of my father's, who was not a Nazi. And was a decent-- he was a decent human being.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told him I needed a place to live. And he said, where do you want to live, boy? And I said, the best house in town is good enough for me. He says, well, what do you mean by that? I said, I lived in barracks and in slums, in squalor for the last four years. I want to live in the best house in this town. Which is the best house in this town?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, well, the best house and the newest house in this town is where the ex-Nazi mayor lived. I said, that's the house I want to live in. Arrange-- I had enough chutzpah by then, I had grown enough by then, to realize where I was in this world. So he says, I'll arrange it for you. I moved into the house. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people came to me and said, well, do you remember I was friends of your mother and I was a friend of your father and I didn't know anything about it and we didn't do anything. And nobody did anything. Nobody was guilty. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Germany there's a word, der Heinzelmaennchen, which means the-- the ghosts were responsible for what happened before 1938 and during 1938. But I had memories of what my father had told me, what my mother had told me, who was involved, directly involved, in our being treated the way we were treated. At that particular time, American troops had moved into Gemunden as occupied territory. They stayed in a place. And I went to the captain, introduced myself to him, told him who I was. And he introduced himself. And he said he was working for-- with the CIC, counterintelligence corps. They were looking for ex-Nazis if I could help them. And I knew quite a few. Since the town was little I knew most of them, if not all of them. And I worked with the American Army for several months, hunting down these people who were Nazis and were actively involved or had been actively involved in hurting us, particularly us, or the Jews, and also other people who were decent German Christian people. I had an incident where I went on patrol with them into a town. And we picked up a man who wanted to hitchhike. And I got into a conversation with him. And somehow we got to talk about Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: I brought up the subject, are there any Jews around? He says, no. There are no Jews around because Hitler killed them all. I said, did he kill 'em all?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, well, he should have killed them all. Those he didn't kill, they should be killed now. And I identified myself as one of those who hadn't been killed. And I was so full of rage at the comment this man had made to me that I asked the sergeant who was in charge of this patrol to leave me alone with this man. And he says, well, I can't do that. I says, well, you can. Just make believe this man does not exist. He left me alone with him. And I believe all the rage and all the anger and all the frustrations I had accumulated from 1932 on to 1945, I released on that man. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I did not kill him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't want to kill him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wanted him to live for the rest of his life [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] remembering me, a Jew, who had survived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I believe, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] really believe, that that incident was a safety valve which was released from within me [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to make me live my life the way I live it today and I have lived since. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The man was released, was put into a hospital. And he recuperated. He was in a similar condition my father was in in 1938, after Kristallnacht, when he was put in the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that when you decided to leave Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had no family there. I went to Germany, went back to Gemunden, forgot to mention that, because I'd hoped my sister had survived. And if she had survived, she would come back for probably the same reason I went back, to find out if I or my father or someone else had survived. Needless to say, she never came to Gemunden. And I have drawn a conclusion from that that she also got killed, she got killed in Stuthoff. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I went back to Neustadt Schleswig-Holstein from Gemunden because I was alone. And I found a friend of mine, one of the two young fellows who had fed me the mashed potatoes with the [NON-ENGLISH] in ghetto. He was in the hospital in Neustadt Schleswig-Holstein where I had been. He had a foot amputated, because he had frozen it during the winter. And I asked him if he would come back to Gemunden with me. He came back with me. And we lived there 'til I immigrated to the United States in 1947. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He immigrated to the United States in 1951 from Gemunden, and went to Kansas City, Missouri, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where he lived for several years, and then to Des Moines, Iowa. We had been like brothers always. Unfortunately, he passed away in 1976. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came to the United States in 1947. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] HIAS brought me over here. I knew I had family in the United States. I didn't know them. I didn't know where they were. I assumed they were in New York. And when I arrived in New York, I did some investigating. And needless to say, I found them, brothers and sisters of my father, who lived in New York. Aunt of mine, Aunt Frieda, who took me in, was married. She had no children. They were very caring and very loving to me. And I'm forever grateful for them for giving me a start here in this country. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I went to work as soon as I came. I arrived on a Thursday. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I got myself a job the following Monday. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Guess what I was doing, what I did? Repairing automobiles, the thing I had learned and survived with in Kaiserwald, in Riga. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] after a while, I wasn't too much interested in it anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted an education, which I didn't have. I left. But now I am-- at the age of 13, it was the end of my schooling. And I also needed to make a living. In any event, I went to night school in New York and learned how to speak English. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I quit being an auto mechanic [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] into [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the meat business. I had an uncle who was in the meat business, who-- who convinced me that that's the work for me to do. And that's what I did. I started working in several meat shops, butcher shops in New York, learned as much as I could, was always interested in learning the intricacies of everything I was doing, did well. And in 1949, I was-- I received a letter from the President of the United States that I was drafted in the American Army. Spoke very little English. I spoke some English, but not enough to really be good enough. I spent a year in the Army of the United States, was discharged in Fort Benning, Georgia, where I served my active duty. Went back to New York in 1951. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went into business for myself, had a business, a candy store in New York, for approximately a year and a half, a year, and decided I wanted to leave New York. Sold the business, and went to visit with my friend in Kansas City, Missouri. He wanted me to stay there, because we were friends from way back when. And Kansas City, Missouri wasn't my kind of town. I went on and arrived in California in October of '52. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I am very happy I made that move.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1957, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to New York. In 1957 I went to New York and I met my wife. We had a very short courtship [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we were married in September of 1958. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she moved to California with me, came to California with me. In 1959, our first son was born, Kenneth, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who is a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] doctor of molecular biology, today works for the National Institute of Health in Washington DC. He's our oldest son. Mark was born. Our second second son was born in 1961, I believe. And he lives here in California, lives in Los Angeles, near us. He is a computer [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] scientist. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Kenny is married, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] has a son, our grandson, the only grandson we have, Jeremiah, who is 9 and 1/2 years old. And obviously, he's our pride and joy.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife, Jeanine, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an artist. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We're married for 37 years, I think, or is it 38? I'm not sure. Very happily married.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at this point in time, I'm enjoying life. I'm retired. I retired in 1989. And life is good to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's good. Is there just one last thing you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really appreciate the privilege of being able to record this for whatever purpose, for other people in the future to know that Holocaust was real and not a figment of some people's imagination.

speaker: interviewer
text: We thank you for giving your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
